DocScript Mastery
RoleInside Sales Agent
Module05 of 06
For New Inside Sales Agents · The Fusion Team

Master your voice.

Five segments. Start with the framework, master the scripts, practice live with the browser microphone, then study a real call from our team with annotations. Headphones recommended.

← Back to onboarding
Script Mastery · Segment 1 of 5

The framework.

Why questions beat scripts. Five short videos from the scripts coach showing you how to listen, lead, and uncover what the seller actually needs.

i
Foundation

Why Scripts Are Dead

Two purposes of every conversation. Why scripted reads fail. The case for questions over statements.

~9 min
Video 01 · Placeholder
"Why Scripts Are Dead"
Replace this block with your <iframe> from YouTube unlisted.
Key takeaways
  • Every conversation has one of two purposes: exchange information or impression management. Know which one you are in.
  • Scripts fail because they sound like a regurgitation. People hear "salesy" within five seconds.
  • The purpose of a script is control of the conversation. Questions do that better than statements while building rapport.
  • Logic makes people think. Emotion makes them act. Questions drill toward emotion.
Phil M Jones Magic Words
"I'm not sure if it's for you, but..."

From Exactly What to Say for Real Estate Agents

"I'm not sure if it's for you, but we have helped two other owners on your block sell over asking this quarter. Worth a quick conversation?"

Why it works: The opener removes pressure. The listener's brain hears "no obligation," which spikes curiosity instead of resistance. Use this on guarded sellers in the first 30 seconds.

Decision Scenario · 01
30 seconds into a call. Seller is guarded. You need to break through.
Look, I'm getting calls like this every day. Who is this and what do you want?
What do you say next?
Why this matters

Homework · Video 1
Practice on your family before you practice on a seller.
  1. For the next 24 hours, every time someone in your house tells you something, respond with "Tell me more about that" instead of giving your opinion.
  2. Write down three statements you would normally make on a call. Convert each into a question.
  3. Re-read the SERHANT Expireds script. Underline every statement vs. every question. Note which dominates.
ii
The Toolbelt

The Seven Question Categories

Inform, pattern interrupt, pause, discover motivation, reciprocity, scaling, isolate objections.

~11 min
Video 02 · Placeholder
"The Question Categories"
Replace this block with your <iframe> from YouTube unlisted.
Key takeaways
  • Inform: "Would you believe me if I told you..."
  • Pattern interrupt: "Hello, this is a cold call. Would you like to hang up?"
  • Pause: "Could you tell me more about that?"
  • Discover motivation: "What would [outcome] do for you and your family?"
  • Reciprocity: "How can I be helpful to you today?"
  • Scaling (1 to 10): Forces a number. Then drill: "What is keeping it from being a 10?"
  • Isolate objections: "What didn't we cover that you hoped we would?"
Phil M Jones Magic Words
"How open-minded would you be about..."

From Exactly What to Say for Real Estate Agents

"How open-minded would you be about having Beatriz come over for fifteen minutes to give you her honest take on the market?"

Why it works: Nobody wants to admit they are closed-minded. This phrase makes "yes" the easier answer.

"Just imagine what your weekend looks like in six months once this property is sold and you are settled into the next chapter."

Why it works: The brain pictures the scenario you paint. Once they emotionally inhabit the outcome, they want to move toward it.

Decision Scenario · 02
Eight minutes into a call with a motivated seller. They told you they want to move to Florida next spring.
Yeah, we definitely want to sell this place. Florida is calling.
Which question gets you the most actionable information?
Why this matters

Homework · Video 2
Build your own question toolbelt.
  1. Take the seven categories. For each, write a version in your own words.
  2. Pick one category that feels weakest. Use it five times in your next mock call session.
  3. Listen to Michael's recorded call. Identify which question categories he used and which he missed.
iii
The Rules

What to Avoid & The Six Skills

Why yes/no questions kill conversations. Why "why" triggers defensiveness.

~3 min
Video 03 · Placeholder
"What to Avoid + Six Skills"
Replace this block with your <iframe> from YouTube unlisted.
Key takeaways
  • Avoid yes/no questions. They end the conversation.
  • Avoid "why" questions. Use "what" or "how" instead.
  • The Six Skills: Open-ended questions, Listen, Confirm, Self-discovery, Reward answers, Control the conversation.
Phil M Jones Magic Words
"What makes you say that?"

From Exactly What to Say for Real Estate Agents

Seller: "Your commission seems too high."
You: "What makes you say that?"

Why it works: This reveals the real objection without making the seller defensive.

"Before you make your mind up, let's just run through what is involved one more time so you know exactly what you are saying no to."

Why it works: A reset button without sounding pushy. Buys you another minute.

Decision Scenario · 03
The seller just told you they are going to relist with the same agent. A family friend.
Tom is family. We have used him for 20 years. I owe him another shot.
What do you ask next?
Why this matters

Homework · Video 3
Find your "why" habit.
  1. For the next two days, every time you catch yourself about to say "why," pause and rephrase as "what" or "how."
  2. Review your five most recent mock calls. Count every yes/no question. Goal: cut that number in half next session.
iv
The Roleplay

Beatriz × the scripts coach · Discovering Motivation

Beatriz plays a "just browsing" buyer. the scripts coach demonstrates curiosity moves a cold lead into a real conversation.

~10 min
Video 04 · Placeholder
"Beatriz × the scripts coach · The Motivation Roleplay"
Replace this block with your <iframe> from YouTube unlisted.
Key takeaways
  • Beatriz opens cold: "Just browsing." the scripts coach doesn't accept the surface answer.
  • Don't accept face value. Most people who reach out have already half-decided.
  • the scripts coach stays curious, not judgmental. Does not pitch SERHANT. until he understands what Beatriz wants.
  • When she reveals investment property interest, the scripts coach pivots to multiple strategies.
  • The close uses the two time appointment frame.
Phil M Jones Magic Words
"When would be a good time to...?"

From Exactly What to Say for Real Estate Agents

"When would be a good time for Beatriz to come by and take a look at the property in person?"

Why it works: The phrasing assumes there will be a good time. Removes "no" as an option. The biggest objection ("I don't have time") never gets a chance to surface.

Decision Scenario · 04
A seller said they are "just browsing." You followed up and learned they are considering selling to buy investment property. They went quiet.
...I don't know. It's a big decision. I'd need to think about it.
What is the strongest move?
Why this matters

Homework · Video 4
Watch Beatriz get out of the way.
  1. Re-watch the roleplay with the sound off. Watch her body language.
  2. Write down the exact sequence of questions the scripts coach uses, in order.
  3. Practice the sequence on a teammate. Use only questions.
v
The Close

Scaling Questions in Action · Rachel's Call

the scripts coach uses the 1 to 10 scaling question, does the math live, closes the appointment in under four minutes.

~5 min
Video 05 · Placeholder
"Scaling Questions · Rachel's Call"
Replace this block with your <iframe> from YouTube unlisted.
Key takeaways
  • Rachel's motivation: her parents' health is declining. the scripts coach anchors on this then converts to action.
  • The scaling question gets Rachel to 6.
  • the scripts coach does the math live: 6 = six months out. Closing takes 30 to 45 days. Finding the right house takes 6 to 8 weeks. So you need to start now.
  • The close uses two specific times.
  • When Rachel hedges, the scripts coach reframes: "Helping people get closer to their parents is not a waste of my time."
Phil M Jones Magic Words
"The good news is..."

From Exactly What to Say for Real Estate Agents

"The good news is we already know what didn't work last time. So when Beatriz walks in, she is starting with that intel, not guessing."

Why it works: Reframes a negative into a positive. The seller's frustration becomes the reason to work with you.

"If I can get Beatriz to come by this week, will you at least hear what she has to say before making your final decision?"

Why it works: Conditional commitment. Once they agree, they have to follow through.

Decision Scenario · 05
Six minutes in. Clear motivation (downsizing). You used a scaling question. They said 6 out of 10.
Yeah, I guess we are a 6. We're definitely thinking about it, but we don't need to move fast.
What is your next move?
Why this matters

Final Homework · Across all 5 videos
Build your call toolkit. This is permanent.
  1. Create a one-page cheat sheet. Top: your seven question categories with your own examples. Middle: your six favorite Phil Jones magic words. Bottom: the scaling-question math.
  2. Take three of your own recent mock calls. Count: how many statements vs. questions?
  3. Submit your cheat sheet to Beatriz before Day 3.
  4. Re-watch Video 4 once more before Wednesday morning.

You are ready.

You met the team. You know the role. You learned the platform. You absorbed five videos and the Phil Jones magic words. Wednesday morning, you pick up the phone. Beatriz will be listening.

Next: Short Script.

The framework you just learned, distilled into a one-page script you can read off the screen on call one.

Start here. Master the basic four-beat call before moving to the full playbook.

01

The Opener

Three lines. Pause after the third. That pause does the work.

Not started
ISA Hello John,
This is [Your Name] with SERHANT.
How are you FEELING today? [ pause ]
Why this works
  • "Feeling" not "doing", it disarms the script-radar most expireds have after 50 cold calls.
  • The pause is the script. Do not fill it. Wait until they answer, even if it's awkward.
  • Say SERHANT with confidence, like a name they should recognize. (Many will.)
  • Never say "Hi, how are you?", that's the call everyone else makes.

Quick check

What word do you use after "How are you" in the opener?

Doing
Feeling
Holding up

After "How are you feeling today?" what do you do?

Pause and wait for them to answer
Immediately introduce the address
Ask another question
02

The Address Reveal

One sentence. Why you're calling. Then a question they must answer.

Not started
ISA I see that 123 Main Street came off the market. I'm interviewing sellers this week, who still want to make a move! Are you still open to selling it?
Coaching
  • "Interviewing sellers" flips the dynamic, they're the candidate, not you.
  • End on a yes/no question. Yes/no commits them; open questions let them drift.
  • If they say "no", don't argue. Move to "what happened there?" instead (see next step).

Quick check

The phrase "I'm interviewing sellers this week" does what?

Makes you sound busy
Buys time before the close
Flips the power dynamic, they're being chosen, not pitched
03

The Diagnostic Question Set

Once they say why it didn't sell, run this sequence. Listen more than you talk.

Not started
ISA Oh, really? Homes like yours typically sell right away. Let me ask you this, I'm not sure if this still makes sense or not, but if you did get an offer with the price and terms you're looking for, is it too late?
Follow-ups (in order) 1. When your previous agent was updating you, what did they say was keeping the home from selling?
2. Did you receive any offers when you had it on the market?
3. What would you say has been the biggest challenge in selling your property?
4. What made you think of selling in the first place?
5. After you sell, what's next?
The mindset
  • You're not selling anything yet. You're diagnosing.
  • The seller talks 70% of this stretch. Take notes, every answer is ammo for Beatriz.
  • "What's next?" is the most important question. It tells you their real motivation (downsize? Florida? divorce? new baby?).
  • If you only have time for 2 questions: ask #3 and #5.

Quick check

What's the most revealing question in the diagnostic set?

"Did you get any offers?"
"After you sell, what's next?"
"What was the biggest challenge?"

What percentage of this part of the call should the seller be talking?

About 30%
About 50%
About 70%
04

The Handoff, Introducing Beatriz

Position the broker, book the appointment, capture the email. Close on calendar, not on email.

Not started
ISA Our team specializes in selling properties that did not sell the first time. Our lead broker Beatriz would love to have a conversation with you. Would you be available for a quick call to meet her tomorrow at 10am, or would Friday at 11am be better?
The two-time close
  • Always offer two specific times, not "when are you free." Two times means they pick, they don't decide whether to meet.
  • Capture the email last, after they've agreed. "Great, what's the best email for the calendar invite?" feels like a logistics question, not an ask.
  • Read back the appointment time before hanging up: "Perfect, so Friday at 11am, calendar invite from Beatriz coming to [email]."
  • This is the moment you log everything in FUB: last 4 of phone, what happened, assign to ISA Leads Follow Up.

Quick check

How do you offer the appointment time?

"When are you available this week?"
Two specific times, "tomorrow at 10am or Friday at 11am?"
"Beatriz will call you when she has time"

After the appointment is booked, what's the next FUB action?

Mark as Expired Sale
Assign to ISA Reject
Log call notes with last 4 of phone + outcome, assign to ISA Leads Follow Up

The full Fusion expireds playbook, three openers, 36 follow-up questions, scaling questions, broker positioning, and the two big rebuttals.

05

Three Opener Variations

Same first three lines. Three ways to pivot into the address reveal, pick by gut read.

Not started
Constant, first 3 lines Hello [Client's name],
This is [Your Name] with SERHANT.
How are you FEELING today? [ pause ]
If they ask "From where?" or "Who?" Do NOT stop to explain. Do NOT repeat yourself. Continue the script.
Variation A, Soft Close From the brokerage Serhant. I saw [address] came off the market. I'm interviewing sellers this week, who still wanna make a move! Are you still open to selling it?
Variation B, Direct From the brokerage Serhant. I saw [address] came off the market. What happened there?
Variation C, Curious From the brokerage Serhant. I saw [address] came off the market. I was curious, what happened?
Use A when…

They sound business-like, professional, or like they expect a sales call. The "interviewing sellers" frame fits their world.

Use B when…

They sound tired, frustrated, or short. "What happened there?" gets them venting, and you want them venting.

Use C when…

They sound guarded or suspicious. "I was curious" lowers the temperature, you're not pitching, you're just wondering.

Quick check

If the seller cuts in with "Who is this from?", what do you do?

Stop and re-introduce yourself slowly
Don't stop, keep going with the script
Hang up and try again later
06

Diagnostic Questions, The Full 36

Pick 3-5 per call. Don't run a checklist. Follow what they give you.

Not started

The opening question after they explain why it didn't sell is always:

Opening probe Oh, really? Homes like yours typically sell right away. Let me ask you this, I'm not sure if this still makes sense or not, but if you did get an offer with the price and terms you're looking for, is it too late?

Tier 1, Foundation (use these on every call)

1. When your previous agent was updating you, what did they say was keeping the home from selling?
2. Did you receive any offers when you had it on the market?
3. What would you say has been the biggest challenge in selling your property?
4. What made you think of selling in the first place?
5. After you sell, what's next?

Tier 2, Motivation deep-dive

6. What was your original goal?
7. What do you feel didn't go right last time?
8. Is this your forever home?
9. If it sold in 30 days, what would change for you?
10. If I were able to show you a path to get you to that number, how open would you be to having that conversation?
11. What have you learned thus far during this experience?

Tier 3, Price & numbers

12. Do you need to net a certain amount?
13. What was missing in the offers that you did receive?
14. What does that number do, when you get it?
15. I'm curious, what would have happened had you sold it?
16. How important is that number on a scale of 1 to 10?
17. What caused you not to say one? What would make it a 10?
18. What is the number that you would consider selling it for?

Tier 4, Working relationship

19. What will make 'us working together' a great experience?
20. Sellers want 3 things: to sell, as fast as possible, for the most money, with the least amount of stress. Which 2 would you prioritize?
21. How long has it been on the market? Is that in line with what you had in mind? When would you like to sell/close by?
22. What is your 'plan B' if the property doesn't sell?
23. How did the last agent market your home?
24. Are you concerned that if you use the same agent, you might end up having the same result?
25. What would make your experience different from the first time you listed?
26. Who do you see as the 'right buyer'?
27. You mentioned [timeline/date], what about that date is important to you?
28. While we work together, what do you expect from me?

Tier 5, Property & market

29. 'Not a good time', what have you been hearing? Can you tell me more about what you mean?
30. Was the market different then?
31. What is the best feature of your property?
32. What kind of work did you do in the property?
33. What type of feedback did you receive from potential buyers?
34. Is there anything you were hoping to talk about that we haven't yet?
35. 'We are taking it off the market' / 'We've decided not to sell', So your plans have changed?
36. When they tell you "what changed?", is it solvable?
The rule
  • Never run more than 5-6 questions in one call. Listing them all here is so you have options based on what they say.
  • If they're a talker, you might only need 2 questions for the whole call.
  • If they're short, use Tier 1 only.
  • The pricing tier (12-18) is gold, but only ask after they've warmed up.

Quick check

Roughly how many questions should you ask in a single call?

3-6 questions, follow their lead
All 36 in order
Just 1 or 2, get to the appointment fast

Which tier is the goldmine for understanding what closes the deal?

Tier 1 (foundation)
Tier 4 (working relationship)
Tier 3 (price & numbers)
07

Broker Positioning + Scaling Questions

How to introduce Beatriz with weight, and the seven questions that uncover real motivation.

Not started
Introducing the broker Our team specializes in selling properties that did not sell the first time. Our lead broker Beatriz would love to have a conversation with you.

Brokerage pointers, use as needed

  • We are on the Netflix show Owning Manhattan and Million Dollar Listing NY
  • Ryan Serhant is the founder, one of the top brokerages in NYC
  • We're the best at what we do and built a brand that puts more eyes on your property than anyone else
  • We're on track to sell $5 billion in real estate this year
  • (Add more pointers discussed on the call)

Scaling questions, pick 1-2 to drill down on motivation

• What's in Florida (or wherever they mentioned) that you wanna get to?
• Originally, when did you want to get there?
• What's important about waiting a year?
• What's going to happen if you wait a year?
• Is there a number you were looking to net at the end of the day?
• What will you do once you sell?
• Got it, well, based on what I can see, the home looks great. Is it an option to just stay put?
Why scaling questions matter
  • "Stay put" is a trap question, if they say "no, I have to sell," they've just told themselves they're committed.
  • "What's in Florida that you wanna get to?" reframes selling as a means to a dream, not a transaction.
  • Use these AFTER the diagnostic questions, BEFORE introducing Beatriz.

Quick check

What's the purpose of "is it an option to just stay put?"

To talk them out of selling
To make them re-commit to selling out loud
To end the call politely

Approximately how much in sales is the Serhant team on track for this year?

$500 million
$1 billion
$5 billion
08

The Two Big Rebuttals

What to say when they say "I'm staying with my agent" or "We're just going to rent it."

Not started

Rebuttal 1, Relisting with same agent

ISA Are you doing this out of loyalty or because you believe they'll deliver a different result this time?
Alternate phrasing Is it loyalty that's keeping you with them, or confidence that they'll get you a better result now?
Why it lands
  • You're not attacking their agent, you're asking them to name their own reason out loud.
  • "Loyalty" almost never wins this argument once they hear it framed against "results."
  • Stay quiet after you ask. Let them sit with it.

Rebuttal 2, "We're going to rent it"

ISA That can definitely be a smart move. Would it help to see how the rental numbers compare to what you'd walk away with if you sold today?
Why it lands
  • Agree first. "That can definitely be a smart move" disarms them.
  • Then offer information, not a pitch. You're saying "let's compare," not "you're wrong."
  • This is the kind of appointment Beatriz wants, a numbers conversation, not a hard sell.

Quick check

When they say "I'm sticking with my agent," what do you do?

Tell them their agent failed
Apologize and end the call
Ask if it's loyalty or confidence in a different result

When they say "we're going to rent it," your first move is to:

Push back, explain why selling is better
Agree that it can be smart, then offer to compare numbers
Move on to a different topic
Script Mastery · Segment 4 of 5

Mock call.

Five seller objections. For each one, pick the response that would actually move the call forward. Tap an answer to see the rationale. The audio mock is being rebuilt, this is the working version for now.

Turn 01 · The cold open
First five seconds of the call. Seller picks up sounding annoyed.
Yeah, hello? Who is this?
What do you say next?
Turn 02 · The reason
They asked what you want. The opener landed. Now you have to give them a reason.
Okay, I am fine. What is this about?
What do you say next?
Turn 03 · The objection
They are venting about the last agent. Two minutes of frustration. They are giving you everything you need if you let them talk.
The last agent never called me back, never showed it to anyone, basically did nothing for six months.
What do you say next?
Turn 04 · The disqualifier
They mention something that changes everything.
Honestly, my brother in law is going to list it next week. He is a broker out in Long Island.
What do you say next?
Turn 05 · The close
They are qualified. They are open. Now you book it.
Okay, so what is the next step?
What do you say next?

Next: Recorded Call from Our Team.

Hear how a real ISA handled the call that booked the appointment.

Script Mastery · Segment 5 of 5

Recorded call.

An actual call by one of our ISAs that booked an appointment with Beatriz. Listen along with the transcript, the coaching notes show exactly what landed at each beat. Click any timestamp to jump there.

ISA
Michael
Seller
Rod
Property
$1.5M condo, has tenant
Duration
~10 minutes
Outcome
Appointment booked ✓
Use the player to listen to the full call. Click any timestamp in the transcript below to jump there.

Annotated Transcript

Coaching notes highlight what Michael did well at each moment.

Michael ISA
Hello, Rod. Hi, this is Michael with SERHANT.
Why this worksClean, calm opener. Name, brokerage, no hesitation. He owns the call from the first word.
Michael ISA
I actually called you a while ago, I'm not sure if you remember, but I caught you at a bad time. Did I still catch you at a bad time this time?
Why this worksSmart move acknowledging the prior call. Shows persistence + respect. The 'is this still a bad time?' frame gives Rod an easy out, which makes him say yes to staying on the line.
Rod SELLER
No, good chance. Tell me.
Michael ISA
Perfect. Again, my name is Michael and I work in the sales team at SERHANT. I saw the property at [address] came off the market and I'm interviewing sellers this week who still want to make a move. Are you still open to selling it?
Why this worksClassic Variation A from the playbook, 'interviewing sellers' flips the dynamic. Ended on the yes/no commit question. Textbook execution.
Rod SELLER
Yeah, I am very much open to selling it. I don't know if you have any thoughts on the apartment, it's quite unique. It's a nice apartment, lots of light, very nice outdoor space. The main issue, I'm not sure what the issue is, but the maintenance fee is quite high...
Michael ISA
[Lets Rod talk through the whole story, maintenance fees, low traffic, the previous agent's pitch, the tenants, his frustration. Doesn't interrupt.]
Why this worksThis is the most important moment in the call. Michael says nothing for almost two minutes and lets Rod vent. By the time Rod stops, he's already told Michael everything he needs to know, price expectations, what went wrong, the tenant situation, his uncertainty. Listening IS the script here.
Rod SELLER
So you have some offers? Just a very few showings. There have been no offers. There's very little showings. So before you take it on, unless you tell me you're smarter than other people, and have better ways of doing it, I don't know if I'm so inclined to pursue another attempt to sell it. I'll let you do the pitch, but I just wanted to give you my two cents.
Michael ISA
Right, I see. And you said you do have tenants on the property, are they still on the property at this time?
Why this worksDoesn't get defensive when Rod says 'I'll let you do the pitch.' Doesn't pitch. Pivots to a qualifying question, tenants matter for Beatriz to know going in. This is exactly how you handle a skeptical seller: don't sell, gather facts.
Rod SELLER
Yes, they still have a year. Given how slow the sell front was, they asked to extend and I extended them another year. So they have another year now. I'm happy to sell with a tenant, I told them it may change hands and they were okay with that.
Michael ISA
Got it. And at this time, are you still working with an agent?
Why this worksCritical qualifier question. You never want to introduce Beatriz to someone already under contract with another agent, that's a procuring-cause headache. Michael asks it cleanly, no apology.
Rod SELLER
No. I took the apartment down. I'm no longer involved.
Michael ISA
Right. I just wanted to make sure you're not affiliated with that agent anymore. And I'm curious, when your last agent was updating you, what did they say was keeping the home from selling?
Why this worksTier 1 diagnostic question from the playbook, straight off question #1. Smooth pivot from qualifying to diagnosing.
Rod SELLER
She didn't really know. The surprise was, I didn't know either.
Michael ISA
And what was the feedback you received from the buyers who visited the property?
Why this worksPlaybook question #33, buyer feedback. This is the question Beatriz will care most about because it tells her what the marketing was missing.
Rod SELLER
Good question. It was always sort of like, 'hey, we like it, we'll come back' and there was never a follow up. It was kind of weird.
Michael ISA
I'm just curious, what made you think of selling it in the first place?
Why this worksPlaybook question #4, motivation. The most important question of the whole call. Notice he says 'I'm just curious' as the lead-in, disarms it from feeling like an interrogation.
Rod SELLER
I think this is a good property. I just don't like to own in New York City right now.
Michael ISA
I see. And for this time, what is the number that you'd consider selling it for?
Why this worksPlaybook question #18, the price. He asked this AFTER the motivation question, not before. That's the right order. Asking price too early feels transactional; asking after motivation feels collaborative.
Rod SELLER
We put it for $1.5M, but who knows. I had an appraisal when I did a refi in 2018, the appraisal was $1.325M. Because the traction has been so terrible I took it down to $1.5M, but who the hell knows. It should be more, but I just don't know anymore.
Michael ISA
Okay. Well, I'm on the sales team and our team specializes in selling properties that didn't sell the first time, just like this one. Our lead broker, her name is Beatriz, would love to have a conversation with you. Would you be available tomorrow at 11 AM your time, just to discuss how we could help you sell your property?
Why this worksTextbook broker introduction. 'Specializes in selling properties that didn't sell the first time', exact playbook line. Offered ONE specific time (next time, offer two, but Friday at 11am landed).
Rod SELLER
I'm willing to have a conversation. But are you going to relay any of my thoughts? I'm only interested in having a conversation if it's a constructive conversation. I don't want to spend another 20 minutes explaining everything I just explained to you.
Michael ISA
Yeah, yeah. I'll relay this. So she won't be like, you won't be repeating it again to her on the conversation.
Why this worksThis is the close. Rod tested him with a real objection ('I don't want to repeat myself') and Michael handled it perfectly, promised to brief Beatriz so the meeting starts at a different starting line. This single sentence is why he got the appointment.
Rod SELLER
Ideally, if you guys have people interested in studios, one bedrooms, lofts, duplex lofts, whatever, or something adjacent, feel free to coordinate a visit.
Michael ISA
Alright, I'll relay that to her as well. We're going to do this one. Excellent. And again, my name is Michael with SERHANT. Thank you so much, I appreciate it. And thank you for the conversation.
Why this worksConfirmed the appointment, restated his name, ended warmly. Now this lead gets logged in FUB: last 4 of Rod's number + 'Friday 11am with Beatriz, has tenant for 1yr, $1.5M price' + assigned to ISA Leads Follow Up.

The 5 Things Michael Did Right

  1. Owned the opener. No hesitation. Name, brokerage, clean. Set the tone from the first word.
  2. Let Rod vent. For nearly two minutes Michael said almost nothing, and by the end Rod had told him the price, the tenant situation, the past agent's failures, and his uncertainty. Listening IS the script.
  3. Didn't pitch when challenged. When Rod said "I'll let you do the pitch," Michael did the opposite, he asked a qualifying question about the tenants. That's discipline.
  4. Asked the right diagnostics in the right order. Agent feedback → buyer feedback → motivation → price. Never price first. Motivation always before money.
  5. Closed the objection. Rod's real worry was "I don't want to repeat myself to Beatriz." Michael promised to brief her, and that single promise won the appointment.
Fusion Team · SERHANT · ISA Onboarding Module 05 · Built for Beatriz Xavier
